text: The surgeon uses a 5mm trocar to mark the site for the feeding jejunostomy, located 50 centimeters from the DJ flexure. The site is selected for future conversion to a transaction point for the Roux-en-Y gastric bypass once the fistula heals.

In a morbidly obese patient, the surgeon marks the site of the feeding jejunostomy with diathermy. Four transfacial fixation sutures are placed above, below, medially, and laterally to secure the jejunum to the parietal wall on all four sides of the feeding jejunostomy.

The surgeon uses a suture passer to place the superior and lateral transfacial fixation sutures, securing the jejunum to the parietal wall. A standard needle holder is used to take the lateral hitching stitch.

The surgeon lifts the jejunum using hitching sutures and creates a small enterotomy at the marked site for the feeding jejunostomy. A purse-string suture is placed around the opening using 3-0 Vicryl sutures.

The surgeon introduces a twelfth French tube for the feeding jejunostomy through the previously marked site using a 5mm trocar. The tube is guided into the distal jejunal limb, extending approximately fifteen to twenty centimeters. The position is confirmed, and a pearl string suture is tied to secure the tube in place.
The surgeon uses a suture passer and needle holder to place medial and inferior hitching sutures, securing the jejunum to the parietal wall at the feeding jejunostomy site.

The surgeon takes an additional fixing suture approximately three centimeters distal to the feeding jejunostomy site to ensure a two-point fixation with a broad base, preventing twisting. After securing the four hitching sutures around the feeding jejunostomy and the distal hitching suture, the neoprotonium is partially deflated, and the sutures are tightened.

Saline is instilled into the distal limb of the feeding jejunostomy to check its patency, confirming free flow. The completed feeding jejunostomy is observed with a broad base of excision designed to prevent torsion.
